title: Android Authentication – Identity and Credential Management

meta.title: Android Authentication Guide – Credential Manager, Passkeys & OAuth (2026)
meta.description: Master user authentication on Android. Learn about the Credential Manager API, implementing Passkeys, and secure OAuth 2.0 flows.

content.value: Authentication on Android has evolved into a 'Passwordless' future. As an Architect, your focus should be on the **Credential Manager API**. This Jetpack library replaces the legacy 'Identity' and 'Google Sign-In' SDKs, providing a single entry point for all authentication methods. It allows Revochamp users to log in using biometric sensors (Face/Fingerprint) via Passkeys, significantly reducing drop-off rates in the Chennai market.

The Credential Manager handles the complexity of storage and retrieval. It supports multiple types of credentials: Passkeys (FIDO2), Google ID Tokens, and traditional Password-based accounts. By using this API, you ensure that user credentials are encrypted and synced across their Google account devices.

Passkeys are a safer, easier replacement for passwords. They use public-key cryptography and are tied to the device's screen lock (Biometrics/PIN). For an Engineering Manager, the benefit is clear: zero phishing risk and no need to manage complex password reset flows for Revochamp's AI services.

When your app needs an extra layer of security—such as before exporting a production-ready AI template—you can use the **BiometricPrompt API**. This allows you to request a fingerprint or face scan without managing the hardware specifics yourself.

faq.question: What happened to the old Google Sign-In SDK?
faq.answer: In 2026, the legacy Google Sign-In SDK is deprecated. All Google identity features have been merged into the Credential Manager. This reduces APK size and provides a consistent experience across all Android versions.

faq.question: Is it safe to store tokens in SharedPreferences?
faq.answer: No. As an Architect, you should never store sensitive Auth Tokens in plain-text SharedPreferences. Use 'EncryptedSharedPreferences' or the 'Android KeyStore' to ensure that tokens are encrypted at rest and cannot be read by rooted devices or other apps.

faq.question: What is an ID Token vs. Access Token?
faq.answer: An ID Token (JWT) is used to verify the user's identity (who they are). An Access Token is used to authorize the app to access specific resources (what they can do, like your Python AI API).

quiz.question: Which Android API is the unified replacement for passwords, Passkeys, and Google Sign-In?
What is the primary security advantage of using Passkeys over passwords?
options: They are immune to phishing and credential stuffing
They work without an internet connection

quiz.question: Which API should be used to request a fingerprint scan for a specific in-app action?
